greeting_etiquette.title: Greeting Etiquette
tips: Always say 'Bonjour' (or 'Bonsoir' in the evening) when entering a shop, restaurant, or any establishment — this is considered essential politeness.
Use 'vous' (formal you) with strangers, service staff, and anyone older; 'tu' is reserved for friends, children, and peers who invite it.
A handshake is standard for first meetings; close friends may greet with 'la bise' (cheek kisses), the number of which varies by region.
When leaving, always say 'Au revoir' or 'Bonne journée/soirée' — departing without a goodbye is considered rude.
In smaller towns, it is common to greet other customers in a waiting room or small shop with a general 'Bonjour'.

restaurant_customs.title: Restaurant Customs
tips: Dinner service in France typically starts at 7:30–8:00 PM; arriving earlier may mean the kitchen is not yet ready.
Service ('le service') is included in the bill by law — tipping is optional, but leaving small change or rounding up is a kind gesture.
Bread is placed directly on the table (not a plate) and is meant to accompany your meal, not as an appetizer with butter.
Asking for modifications to menu items (substitutions, removals) is much less common and may be met with resistance.
Water: ask for 'une carafe d'eau' for free tap water; otherwise you will receive (and be charged for) bottled water.

shopping_norms.title: Shopping Norms
tips: Greet the shopkeeper with 'Bonjour' when entering and 'Au revoir' when leaving — even in large stores.
National sales periods ('soldes') are regulated by the government and occur twice a year (January and June/July).
Many small shops and businesses close between 12:00 PM and 2:00 PM for lunch, especially outside Paris.
Sunday closures are common for non-food shops; supermarkets may open on Sunday mornings in some areas.
Haggling is not customary in French shops, though it may be acceptable at flea markets ('marchés aux puces').

transit_tips.title: Transit Tips
tips: The Paris Métro uses a zone-based system; keep your ticket until you exit as inspectors may check.
TGV (high-speed train) tickets have assigned seats; regional TER tickets often do not.
Validate ('composter') paper tickets for regional trains in the yellow or green machines on the platform before boarding.
Announcements in stations and on trains are typically in French first, then English on major routes.
Strikes ('grèves') affecting transport are not uncommon — check SNCF or RATP websites for service updates.

pharmacy_expectations.title: Pharmacy Expectations
tips: French pharmacists are trained to diagnose and treat minor ailments — describe your symptoms and they will recommend medication.
Pharmacies are identified by a flashing green cross; there is always one open nearby on nights and weekends (duty pharmacy).
Some medications available over-the-counter in other countries require a prescription ('ordonnance') in France.
Pharmacies also sell high-quality skincare and beauty products at competitive prices.

general_social_norms.title: General Social Norms
tips: Speaking even a few words of French is deeply appreciated — always attempt before switching to English.
Ask 'Parlez-vous anglais ?' politely rather than launching directly into English.
The French value privacy — avoid asking personal questions about salary, age, or political preferences with acquaintances.
Meals are a social event and are not rushed; expect dinner to last 1.5–2 hours.
Queue etiquette is important — cutting in line is highly frowned upon.
